rationale: The citing paper uses De Jong et al. (2008) as concrete prior evidence that extreme responding is not only an individual trait but can be shaped by survey design characteristics (specifically question length). This supports the paper's conceptual setup that measurement formats can systematically bias response intensity, which directly motivates testing language-of-scale effects (ACE). The citation also reappears in the discussion to position the contribution within response-style and cross-national measurement research. Although the citing paper does not reuse De Jong et al.'s IRT method, it relies on that work for framing its core measurement-bias argument.
relation_description: The citing paper treats the cited paper as prior empirical grounding that survey instrument features can influence extreme response behavior. It builds its own argument for language-based anchor effects on top of this response-style measurement foundation.
